channel.title: Identifier on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Identifier on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C errno 43 EIDRM — Identifier removed
item.description:
[Solution] C errno 43 EIDRM — Identifier removed

Identifier removed occurs when a system call fails and sets errno to 43. This error indicates that the requested operation cannot be performed due to the specific condition described by EIDRM.

Common Causes

  • Trying to access a System V IPC resource (semaphore, shared memory, message queue) that has been removed.
  • Using a semaphore ID after semctl(IPC_RMID) was called.
  • Accessing a shared memory segment after shmctl(IPC_RMID).
  • Race condition between resource creation and removal.

How to Fix

#include <sys/sem.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <string.h>

int main(void) {
 int semid = semget(IPC_PRIVATE, 1, 0600);
 if (semid == -1) {
 perror("semget");
 return 1;
 }
 semctl(semid, 0, IPC_RMID);
 // Now try to use the removed semaphore
 struct sembuf op = {0, 1, 0};
 if (semop(semid, &op, 1) == -1) {
 fprintf(stderr, "semop failed: %s (errno %d)\n", strerror(errno), errno);
 }
 return 0;
}

Examples

#include <sys/shm.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <errno.h>

int main(void) {
 int shmid = shmget(IPC_PRIVATE, 4096, IPC_CREAT | 0600);
 if (shmid == -1) {
 perror("shmget");
 return 1;
 }
 shmctl(shmid, IPC_RMID, NULL);
 char *ptr = shmat(shmid, NULL, 0);
 if (ptr == (void *)-1 && errno == EIDRM) {
 fprintf(stderr, "Shared memory segment removed\n");
 }
 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • errno-11 EAGAIN — resource unavailable.
  • errno-43 EIDRM — identifier removed (self).
  • errno-13 EACCES — permission denied.

item.title: [Solution] Linux EIDSEQ (errno 43) — Identifier Removed Fix
item.description:
Linux EIDSEQ (errno 43) — Identifier Removed

EIDSEQ (errno 43) means an IPC identifier (such as a message queue, semaphore, or shared memory segment) has been removed while a process was still referencing it. This error occurs when msgsnd(), msgrcv(), semop(), or shmat() is called on an IPC resource that was deleted by another process or via ipcrm.

Common Causes

  • An IPC resource was removed with ipcrm while another process was using it
  • The IPC resource was removed via msgctl(), semctl(), or shmctl() with IPC_RMID
  • A process crashed without cleaning up IPC resources, and they were later removed
  • A race condition between resource creation and deletion

How to Fix EIDSEQ

1. Check IPC Resource Status

Verify if the IPC resource still exists:

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2300 — Duplicate identifier
item.description:
TS2300 — Duplicate identifier

TS2300 occurs when two declarations in the same scope have the same name. This can happen with variables, functions, classes, or type declarations.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Duplicate variable declaration
let x = 5;
let x = 10; // TS2300: Duplicate identifier 'x'

// Cause 2: Duplicate function declaration
function greet() {}
function greet() {} // TS2300

// Cause 3: Conflicting type declarations
interface Config {
 port: number;
}
interface Config {
 host: string; // TS2300: duplicate identifier
}

// Cause 4: Conflicting with global type
let Array = [1, 2, 3]; // TS2300: 'Array' already defined

How to Fix

Fix 1: Rename one of the declarations

let x1 = 5;
let x2 = 10;

Fix 2: Use unique names or modules

// In separate modules
// file1.ts
export const config1 = {};

// file2.ts
export const config2 = {};

Fix 3: Merge interface declarations

interface Config {
 port: number;
 host: string;
}

Fix 4: Avoid shadowing globals

const myArray = [1, 2, 3]; // use different name

Related Errors

  • TS2309: Variable is declared but never read — unused declaration.
  • TS2391: Function redeclaration — function redeclaration.
  • TS2464: Circular definition of import alias — circular imports.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2464 — Class Can Only Implement an Identifier
item.description:
TS2464 — A class can only implement an identifier/qualified-name

TS2464 occurs when you use implements with an expression that isn’t a valid identifier or qualified name. You can only implement named interfaces or classes, not object literals, unions, or computed values.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Implementing an object literal
class MyClass implements { name: string; age: number } { // TS2464
 name = "";
 age = 0;
}

// Cause 2: Implementing a union type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: string; }
class MyClass implements A | B { // TS2464
 a = "";
}

// Cause 3: Implementing an expression
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {} // TS2464

// Cause 4: Implementing an inline type without a name
class MyClass implements { id: number } {} // TS2464

Solutions

Fix 1: Define an interface and implement it

// Wrong
class MyClass implements { name: string } {}

// Correct
interface Named {
 name: string;
}
class MyClass implements Named {
 name = "";
}

Fix 2: Only implement one interface (not a union)

// Wrong
class MyClass implements A | B {}

// Correct — implement both separately
class MyClass implements A, B {
 a = "";
 b = "";
}

Fix 3: Use interface reference instead of inline type

// Wrong
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {}

// Correct
interface HasX {
 x: number;
}
class MyClass implements HasX {
 x = 0;
}

Fix 4: Implement named type aliases

type Printable = { print(): void };
class MyClass implements Printable {
 print() { console.log("printed"); }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Trying to implement a generic type without instantiation
interface Container<T> { value: T; }
class MyClass implements Container { // TS2464 — missing type argument
 value = "";
}

// Fix:
class MyClass implements Container<string> {
 value = "";
}

// Example 2: Implementing a mapped type
type Options = { [key: string]: string };
class Config implements Options {} // TS2464 — can't implement mapped type directly

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements interface — implements but missing members.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] uuid Parse Error Fix
item.description:
uuid Parse Error

Fix uuid parsing errors. Handle invalid format, version mismatch, and generation..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the crate/library is properly configured

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

use anyhow::Result;

fn do_something() -> Result<()> {
 // Use proper error handling with Result and ?
 Ok(())
}

Fix 3: Add timeout and retry logic

use std::time::Duration;

// Add timeout for network operations
let result = tokio::time::timeout(
 Duration::from_secs(30),
 do_operation(),
).await;

Examples

use std::error::Error;

fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn Error>> {
 // Operation that may fail
 let result = do_work()?;
 println!("{:?}", result);
 Ok(())
}

Related Errors

  • Connection Refused — connection refused
  • Timed Out — request timed out
  • IO Error — I/O error

item.title: Invalid character in name
item.description:
What This Error Means

An Invalid character in name error occurs when you use characters that aren’t allowed in Haskell identifiers. Haskell has specific rules about what characters can appear in variable and type names.

Common Causes

  • Using special characters in identifiers
  • Unicode characters in code
  • Reserved words used as identifiers
  • Invalid operator characters

How to Fix

-- WRONG: Invalid character in identifier
my-var = 10 -- Invalid character: -

-- CORRECT: Use valid characters
myVar = 10

-- WRONG: Using reserved word
data = [1, 2, 3] -- 'data' is reserved

-- CORRECT: Use different name
myData = [1, 2, 3]

Examples

-- Example 1: Hyphen in name
my-function = 10 -- Invalid character

-- Example 2: Special character
var@name = 10 -- Invalid character: @

-- Example 3: Unicode
café = "coffee" -- may cause issues in some compilers

Related Errors

  • Variable not in scope
  • GHC compilation error
